questions.prompt:
(2 points) For two random variables, the expectation of their sum is equal to the sum of their
expected values, and the expectation of their product is equal to the product of their expected
values.
. (2 points) Increasing the size of training datasets always reduces overfitting.
. (2 points) K-means is a supervised learning technique, while K-Nearest Neighbor is an unsu-
pervised learning technique.
. (2 points) Increasing the batch size when using mini-batch Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
always improves training.
. (2 points) In terms of expressiveness, a 5-layer neural network with only linear activation
functions is equivalent to one single-layer neural network.
. (2 points) Cross validation will guarantee that our model does not overfit.
. (2 points) A logistic regression model is equivalent to a one-layer neural network, which has
a single output unit with sigmoid activation and is optimized with a cross-entropy loss func-
tion.
. (2 points) Suppose we use a simple linear regression model to perform classification, where
the objective is simply the squared-error loss. If we achieve 100% accuracy on the training
set, then the training loss must also be zero.
1.

Problem 2
Multiple Choice Questions (one or more choices are correct)
1. (4 points) Suppose the training error of a neural network with two hidden layers is high.
Which of the following, if done in isolation, has a better-than-tiny chance of reducing the
training error?
A. Training on more data
B. Adding more units to hidden layers
C. Decreasing the number of hidden layers
D. Normalizing the input data

questions.prompt:
(4 points) Which of the following are true for the k-nearest neighbor (k-NN) algorithm?
A. As k increases, the variance usually increases.
B. As k increases, variance and bias usually decrease.
C. As k increases, the bias usually increases.
D. The decision boundary looks less smoother with smaller values of k.
Problem 3
In this question, you want to figure out what makes people click on online advertisements via de-
cision trees. You try a variety of different advertisements and record user behavior in the following
table. The middle three columns (“Animated,” “Popup,” and “Colorful”) are the features; the last
column, “Clicked,” is the label you want to predict by constructing a decision tree.
Table 1: The recorded 8 data points.
Advertisement || Animated? | Popup? | Colorful? || Clicked
1 | Yes | Yes | No | No
2 | No | Yes | Yes || No
3 | No | Yes | No || No
4 | No | No | Yes || Yes
5 | Yes | No | Yes || Yes
6 | Yes | No | No | Yes
7 | No | No | Yes || Yes
8 | No | No | No || No
1. (4 points) What is the entropy H(Clicked) at the root node? [Note: Your expression can con-
tain logarithms and fractions.]
2. (6 points) Which feature should you split on at the root of the decision tree to maximize the
information gain? Write an expression for the information gain of the best split. [Note: Your
expression can contain logarithms and fractions. ]

Problem 4
Consider a simple XNOR dataset consisting of four data points. Each data point has two binary
features x, and x2, and the class label would be 1 if x, = x2 and vice versa. We also annotate each
data point by a unique identifier (the first column of Table 2), which is unrelated to the learning
process and only for ease of your reference.
Table 2: The XNOR dataset consisting of four points.
ID | a | x2 | y
a |0|0|1
b |0|1|0
c |1|0|0
d|1|1|1
1. (5 points) Consider a 1-Nearest Neighbor (1-NN) algorithm with leave-one-out cross validation.
Compute the top-1 nearest neighbor for each of these four points and explain your answer. Is
1-NN able to accurately classify the dataset? [Hint: you can think of it as running the 1-NN
algorithm four times, where each time we leave one point out as the test point and use the
remaining three points as the training set.]
2. (5 points) Suppose you are given a simple linear classifier with weights w1, we, and bias b.
The classifier outputs 9 = wir, + w222 + b, where it would predict class label to 1 if 9 > 0
and predict 0 if § < 0. By considering all of these four points, write the set of inequalities
which these weights must satisfy so that the classifier can accurately classify the dataset.
Does a feasible solution (or a set of solutions) exist for such constraints? If yes, give a set of
the weights and biases which can correctly classify all four examples; if not, explain why that
is the case.

Problem 5
You want to train a neural network to recognize the age and sex based on the face image. Your
training data consists of grayscale 32 x 32 pixel images. The training labels include the age and sex
of each person. Your neural network consists of an input layer with 32 x 32 = 1,024 units, a hidden
layer with 2,048 units, and an output layer with 2 units. You use the ReLU activation function for
the hidden units and no activation function for the outputs (or inputs). You train your network
with the cost function J = $|ly — z||?. Please use the following notations to answer questions:
e x is a training image (input) vector with a 1 component appended to the end (as a bias), y is

ponent to the end of h as the bias term).
e V is the weight matrix mapping the input layer to the hidden layer; g = Vx.
e W is the weight matrix mapping the hidden layer to the output layer; z = Wh.
